places.name: Camaguey

visits.text: Camagüey (UNESCO since 2008) is Cuba's third-largest city. Its labyrinthine street plan - unique among colonial cities in the Americas - was designed in the 17th century to confuse pirates who regularly plundered the town. It's easy to get lost: winding lanes, dead ends, hidden squares. The tinajones, large earthenware jars 1 to 2 meters in diameter, used to collect rainwater, are the city's symbol - there were more than 16,000 of them in the 19th century.

Plaza San Juan de Dios, one of Cuba's best-preserved squares, dates from the 18th century. The former San Juan de Dios hospital (1728), with its arcaded courtyard and adjoining church, has been restored and houses a colonial architecture museum. Plaza del Carmen features life-size bronze sculptures of everyday characters: a woman reading, an old man on a bench, a peasant woman with her baskets.

activities.text: Walking tour through the labyrinthine historic center. Bicitaxis (bicycle taxis) offer guided circuits among the main squares and churches. The Iglesia de la Merced (18th century) has visitable funeral catacombs.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Cuba Circuit: Havana, Viñales, Trinidad, Santiago & Varadero

map_keywords: Plaza San Juan de Dios Camagüey
Iglesia Nuestra Señora de la Merced Camagüey

places.name: Cayo Guillermo

visits.text: A long morning drive from Trinidad to the cayos via Santa Clara (about 4h30). The northern coast islands (Coco, Guillermo) are reached via a 17-km causeway built over the open sea. Cayo Guillermo is wilder than its neighbour Cayo Coco, and was the favourite hideout of a famous American writer who came here to fish for marlin in the 1940s and 1950s.

A full day on the cayos. Beyond Playa Pilar, neighbouring Cayo Coco — linked by road — has its own fine-sand beaches fringed with palm trees and flamingo colonies in the interior lagoons. The El Bagá natural park showcases Cuba's endemic wildlife in a mangrove setting.

activities.text: Arrive in the early afternoon, settle in, then head straight to Playa Pilar — the beach many Cubans call the most beautiful in the country: blinding white sand, dunes up to 15 metres high (the tallest in the Caribbean), and shallow turquoise water. The beach stays quiet because it sits at the far western tip of the island.
Snorkelling on the coral reef off Cayo Guillermo, reached by organised boat excursion (reefs lie 200–500 m offshore). There is also the option to swim with dolphins at a natural dolphinarium.

days_plan.itin_title: Cuba: Culture & Beach — Eco-Villages, Cenotes, Secret Cayos & Colonial Towns
Cuba: Culture & Beach — Eco-Villages, Cenotes, Secret Cayos & Colonial Towns

map_keywords: Parque Natural El Bagá Cayo Coco

visits.text: The northern cayos of Villa Clara (Las Brujas, Ensenachos, Santa María) are linked to the mainland by a 48-km pedraplén that crosses the sea. Cayo Las Brujas offers white sand beaches with transparent waters, in a less concrete-heavy setting than Varadero. The seabed shelters corals, tropical fish and rays.

activities.text: Beach day and snorkeling on the cayos. The pedraplén itself is worth the journey: the road runs straight over the turquoise water, with 360° ocean views.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Cuba Circuit: Havana, Viñales, Trinidad, Santiago & Varadero

visits.text: Cienfuegos (UNESCO since 2005), nicknamed "The Pearl of the South," is the only Latin American city founded by the French under Spanish rule. On April 22, 1819, Louis de Clouet and 36 Bordeaux settlers laid out the first streets around Parque José Martí. The perfect grid plan, inspired by the Enlightenment, remains intact. Around the square: the Teatro Tomás Terry (1890), inspired by Italian theaters, the neoclassical cathedral with French stained glass, and the Palacio Ferrer (1918) whose dome offers a bay view.

The Palacio de Valle, in the Punta Gorda district, was built between 1913 and 1917 by Italian architect Alfredo Colli for Asturian sugar magnate Acisclo del Valle. The building blends Moorish, Gothic, Byzantine and Baroque styles - like a Moroccan kasbah transplanted to the Caribbean. Spanish, Italian and Cuban artisans worked together on it. After the 1959 Revolution, plans to convert it into a casino were abandoned. Climb to the rooftop terrace: panoramic view over the entire Cienfuegos bay.

activities.text: Stroll along the Paseo del Prado of Cienfuegos - the longest in Cuba at 900 meters - then along the Malecón facing the bay. Cienfuegos Bay is one of the world's largest natural bays.

days_plan.itin_title: Cuba: Culture & Beach — Eco-Villages, Cenotes, Secret Cayos & Colonial Towns
Complete Cuba Circuit: Havana, Viñales, Trinidad, Santiago & Varadero

places.name: Havana

visits.text: Havana was founded in 1519 by the Spanish, and its historic center has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1982. Start at Plaza de la Catedral: the San Cristóbal Cathedral, completed in 1777 by the Jesuits, features an asymmetrical baroque facade - its two towers aren't the same width, a rare occurrence in colonial architecture. The square is framed by the Palacio del Marqués de Aguas Claras and the Palacio del Conde de Lombillo, both from the 18th century.

Walk down Calle Obispo, a 460-meter pedestrian artery connecting Plaza de Armas to Parque Central. Along the way, step inside the Taquechel pharmacy, preserved in its 19th-century state with porcelain jars and mahogany furniture. Plaza de Armas, Havana's oldest square (late 16th century), hosts the Palacio de los Capitanes Generales (1776) - its wooden paving stones were laid to muffle the noise of carts that disturbed the governor.

The Castillo de la Real Fuerza, built between 1558 and 1577 after the sacking of Havana by French corsair Jacques de Sores in 1555, is the oldest stone fortress in the Americas. Atop its tower, the bronze weathervane La Giraldilla (1632) has become the symbol of Havana.

El Capitolio, constructed from 1926 to 1929 by architect Eugenio Rayneri Piedra, measures 207 meters long. Its dome rises to 92 meters - the world's third-highest at the time. Inside, the bronze Statue of the Republic (14.6 m, 30 tons) was then the world's third-largest covered statue. Beneath the dome, a 25-carat diamond set in the floor marks kilometer zero for all Cuban roads. The Paseo del Prado, laid out in 1772 and redesigned in 1928 by French urban planner Forestier, connects the Capitol to the Malecón.

Plaza de la Revolución, designed in the 1920s by French urban planner Forestier, covers 72,000 square meters. The José Martí Memorial (1953-1958), shaped like a five-pointed star carved from gray marble from the Isle of Youth, rises to 109 meters - the highest point in Havana. Take the elevator or climb 567 steps to the observation deck. At its base, the white marble statue of a seated, pensive Martí measures 18 meters. On the Interior Ministry facade, the giant portrait of Che reproduces Korda's 1960 photograph.

The Museum of the Revolution occupies the former presidential palace (1913), used by Cuban presidents until 1959. The Hall of Mirrors, inspired by Versailles, contrasts sharply with the guerrilla warfare displays. At the rear, the Granma Pavilion houses the 18-meter yacht that carried 82 revolutionaries from Mexico in 1956.

Plaza Vieja, laid out in 1559, served as a marketplace, a bullfighting arena in the 18th century, then a parking lot in the 1950s - before being restored from the 1990s onward. The surrounding houses span four centuries of architecture. The Camera Obscura installed in the former Palacio Gómez Vila offers a 360° view of the city.

The Rum Museum, housed in an 18th-century colonial mansion, traces the production of Cuban rum: molasses fermentation, distillation in copper stills, aging in white oak barrels. An animated scale model reproduces the workings of a 19th-century sugar mill.

Plaza de San Francisco, facing the harbor, is dominated by the Convento de San Francisco de Asís (1608-1738), whose 42-meter tower served as a lookout for ships. The church, deconsecrated, now hosts classical music concerts thanks to its remarkable acoustics. The Lonja del Comercio (1909), the former stock exchange, features a neoclassical facade adorned with a dome crowned by a statue of Mercury.

activities.text: The drive from the airport area takes about 30 minutes. Take your time strolling toward the historic center at your own pace.

Late afternoon, walk along the Malecón, an 8-km promenade built from 1901 to 1958. Locals call it "the world's longest sofa": at sunset, hundreds of Habaneros sit on the seawall facing the ocean. Every evening at 9 PM, a cannon shot rings out from La Cabaña fortress - a tradition perpetuating the ancient closing of the city walls.
Panoramic tour in a 1950s American car through the Vedado and Miramar districts. These vehicles, kept running by Cuban mechanics since the 1962 embargo, have become a symbol of the country.
Craft market at the Almacenes de San José, a 19th-century port warehouse converted into a covered hall beside the bay.

Street art walk through Callejón de Hamel, a 200-meter alley transformed into an open-air gallery since 1990 by muralist Salvador González. Afro-Cuban frescoes, scrap metal sculptures - on Sundays around noon, percussionists play rumba there.

Final morning: self-guided circuit among the four colonial plazas (Armas, Vieja, San Francisco, Catedral) for details missed on the first pass. Last-minute shopping at the Almacenes de San José. Late afternoon, final sunset over the Malecón.

map_keywords: Castillo de la Real Fuerza Havana

activities.text: Transfer time to the airport area and departure formalities. Allow 30 minutes drive from downtown Havana.

days_plan.itin_title: Cuba: Culture & Beach — Eco-Villages, Cenotes, Secret Cayos & Colonial Towns
Complete Cuba Circuit: Havana, Viñales, Trinidad, Santiago & Varadero

places.name: Las Terrazas

visits.text: Las Terrazas is a 1,000-person eco-village founded in 1968 out of an extraordinary act of ecological repair: after centuries of deforestation caused by coffee plantations and charcoal production, seven million trees — mahogany, cedar, teak — were replanted across 5,000 hectares of hillside terraces carved by hand (hence the name). In 1985, the Sierra del Rosario was designated Cuba's first UNESCO Biosphere Reserve. Today the community, built around an artificial lake, earns 60% of its income from eco-tourism.

Cafetal Buenavista is the ruin of a coffee plantation founded in the early 19th century by French colonists who fled the Haitian Revolution. The stone drying terraces, slave quarters and manor house still stand. At the region's peak, there were 54 plantations like this one — Buenavista is among the oldest survivors.

The Orchid Garden at Soroa, 20 km from Las Terrazas, was created in the 1940s by a Canarian lawyer in memory of his wife and daughter. Spread across 35,000 m² of mountain terraces, it brings together over 700 orchid species — including 250 endemic to Cuba.

activities.text: Swim in the Baños del Río San Juan — a series of natural pools and small waterfalls deep in the tropical forest, 3 km from Las Terrazas. Cool, clear freshwater flows between moss-covered rocks.

Guided hike through the Sierra del Rosario: over 120 bird species live here, including the tocororo — Cuba's national bird, wearing the red, white and blue of the Cuban flag — and the tiny, jewel-bright Cuban tody, a red-and-green endemic found nowhere else on earth.

days_plan.itin_title: Cuba: Culture & Beach — Eco-Villages, Cenotes, Secret Cayos & Colonial Towns

map_keywords: Baños del Río San Juan Las Terrazas

visits.text: Flight Santiago → Havana (~1h), then drive to Matanzas (~1h30). Matanzas, nicknamed "the Athens of Cuba" for its literary and musical tradition (the danzón was born here in 1879), is crossed by the Yumurí and San Juan rivers. Its stone bridges also earned it the nickname "city of bridges." The historic center, with quiet, un-touristy streets, preserves 19th-century neoclassical mansions.

activities.text: The Bellamar Caves, discovered in 1861 by a Chinese worker digging a well, constitute Cuba's oldest tourist site (opened to the public in 1863). The galleries extend 2.5 km at 50 meters depth, with crystalline formations 40,000 years old: stalagmites, stalactites and an underground lake.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Cuba Circuit: Havana, Viñales, Trinidad, Santiago & Varadero

visits.text: A long driving day: leave Viñales early, cross the plains of Pinar del Río and skirt Havana on the national motorway (about 5h30 on the road). You'll arrive at Playa Girón in the early afternoon. This coastal village takes its name from Gilberto Girón, a French pirate who was beheaded here in the 17th century — though it is better known for the failed Bay of Pigs landing on 17 April 1961, when 1,400 CIA-trained Cuban exiles tried to topple the Castro regime and were routed in 72 hours. The museum displays photos, weapons and documents from this pivotal Cold War episode.

La Cueva de los Peces, halfway between Playa Larga and Playa Girón, is Cuba's deepest cenote: 70 metres of crystal-clear freshwater filling a tectonic fissure connected to the sea by underwater tunnels. Tropical fish drift through the clear water. The adjacent beach is ideal for snorkelling directly from the shore.

activities.text: Late-afternoon swim in the turquoise waters of the Bay of Pigs. Underwater visibility here ranges from 20 to 40 metres, with water temperatures between 22°C and 29°C depending on the season.

days_plan.itin_title: Cuba: Culture & Beach — Eco-Villages, Cenotes, Secret Cayos & Colonial Towns

visits.text: Remedios, founded in 1514, is one of Cuba's oldest towns — and one of its least known. Its Plaza Martí is the only square in Cuba with two churches facing each other. The Iglesia San Juan Bautista (16th century, rebuilt in the 18th) hides a remarkable secret: an altar covered entirely in gold leaf, forgotten under layers of paint for centuries and only rediscovered during a restoration in the 1940s.

The Museo de las Parrandas documents the 'parrandas' — raucous street festivals held every 24 December since 1820, in which two rival neighbourhoods (San Salvador and El Carmen) compete by building enormous illuminated floats and staging fireworks battles. A tradition unique to Cuba, now inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list.

activities.text: Wander through Remedios, a town that mass tourism has barely touched. Low pastel-coloured houses, flower-filled courtyards, rocking chairs on front porches. The atmosphere of Trinidad, without the crowds.

days_plan.itin_title: Cuba: Culture & Beach — Eco-Villages, Cenotes, Secret Cayos & Colonial Towns
Complete Cuba Circuit: Havana, Viñales, Trinidad, Santiago & Varadero

visits.text: Santa Clara is Che Guevara's city in Cuba. The Mausoleum and Memorial, inaugurated in 1988, houses the remains of Che and 29 of his companions who fell in Bolivia in 1967, repatriated in 1997. The 6.7-meter bronze statue towers over an underground museum tracing his life: handwritten letters, original beret, guerrilla photos.

The Tren Blindado Monument marks the exact spot where, on December 29, 1958, Che's guerrillas derailed an armored train sent by Batista with 408 soldiers and weapons. The four wagons, left in place with a bulldozer used to tear up the rails, form an open-air museum. This decisive victory precipitated the regime's fall - Batista fled Cuba two days later.

activities.text: Walk through Parque Vidal, Santa Clara's central square bordered by colonial buildings and the Teatro La Caridad (1885). During segregation, a low wall separated the park's center (for Whites) from the perimeter (for Blacks) - the wall was demolished after the Revolution.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Cuba Circuit: Havana, Viñales, Trinidad, Santiago & Varadero

places.name: Santiago De Cuba

visits.text: Santiago de Cuba, founded in 1514, is the country's second city (~500,000 inhabitants) and the island's former capital. Parque Céspedes is the heart of the city: the Nuestra Señora de la Asunción Cathedral (Cuba's first cathedral, rebuilt after the 1932 earthquake), the Casa de Diego Velázquez (1516-1530, probably Cuba's oldest colonial dwelling) and the Hotel Casa Granda (1914) surround it.

Walk down Calle Heredia, Santiago's cultural artery. The Casa de la Trova, founded in 1968, is the temple of traditional Cuban music: son, bolero, guaracha. Musicians play there every afternoon and evening. Santiago is considered the birthplace of son cubano - the ancestor of salsa - born in the Oriente mountains in the early 20th century.

The Castillo de San Pedro de la Roca (El Morro), a UNESCO site since 1997, was designed in 1637 by Italian engineer Giovanni Battista Antonelli to protect the bay from pirates. Built on a steep rocky promontory, the fortress unfolds over several terrace levels connected by flights of steps. It's the best-preserved example of 17th-century Spanish-American military architecture. At sunset, the view of the narrow bay entrance channel is striking.

The Moncada Barracks, the country's second-strongest military installation under Batista, was attacked on July 26, 1953 by a group of 93 young revolutionaries led by Fidel Castro - a failed attack that cost most of them their lives but marked the beginning of the Cuban Revolution. Bullet impacts are still visible on the facade. Converted into a school after 1959, it houses the Museo Histórico 26 de Julio: photos, weapons, uniforms and period documents.

The Santa Ifigenia Cemetery, Cuba's second-most important cemetery after Havana's, houses José Martí's mausoleum (1951) - a 23-meter octagonal tower oriented so sunlight illuminates the coffin draped in the Cuban flag. The changing of the guard takes place every 30 minutes.

activities.text: The drive from Camagüey is lengthy (~5h). Late afternoon arrival. Evening stroll through the steep downtown streets, where music pours from every door and window. Santiago is more Caribbean, more mixed, and more musical than Havana - the influence of French colonists from Saint-Domingue (arrived in 1804 after Haiti's independence) is palpable.
Visit the Museo Emilio Bacardí, founded in 1899 - Cuba's oldest museum. Archaeological collections (Egyptian mummies, Taíno artifacts), Cuban paintings and independence war memorabilia.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Cuba Circuit: Havana, Viñales, Trinidad, Santiago & Varadero
Complete Cuba Circuit: Havana, Viñales, Trinidad, Santiago & Varadero

map_keywords: Casa de la Trova Santiago de Cuba
Castillo del Morro Santiago de Cuba
Santa Ifigenia Cemetery Santiago de Cuba
Emilio Bacardí Museum Santiago de Cuba

places.name: Trinidad

visits.text: Trinidad (UNESCO since 1988), founded in 1514 by conquistador Diego Velázquez, is Cuba's best-preserved colonial town. Plaza Mayor, paved with irregular cobblestones, is surrounded by 18th and 19th-century mansions built by the sugar barons. The Museo Romántico (Palacio Brunet, 1812) preserves period furnishings: mahogany furniture, Bohemian crystal chandeliers, Sèvres porcelain.

The San Francisco de Asís convent, built in 1813, houses the Museo de la Lucha Contra Bandidos (Museum of the Fight Against Bandits). Its yellow and green bell tower, visible from all over town, is Trinidad's main landmark. Climb the steps for a 360° view over the red-tiled roofs, the Escambray mountains and the Caribbean Sea to the south.

The Valle de los Ingenios (UNESCO), 12 km from Trinidad, covers 225 square kilometers and had over 50 sugar mills in the 19th century with more than 11,000 slaves in 1827. The Torre Manaca Iznaga, built between 1815 and 1830 by the Iznaga family (Creoles of Basque origin), rises 45 meters over seven stories. It served to monitor slaves and to ring the Angelus. Its bells marked the start and end of work. From the top, views over the valley, the sugar mill ruins and the Escambray massif.

Topes de Collantes, a natural park in the Sierra del Escambray at 800 meters altitude, shelters forests of pines and tree ferns in a cool, humid climate. The trail leads to Salto del Caburní, a 62-meter waterfall plunging into a natural pool surrounded by tropical vegetation.

Playa Ancón, 12 km south of Trinidad on the peninsula of the same name, is the finest beach on Cuba's south coast. The white sand stretches for 4 km facing calm, deep blue waters. A coral reef a few hundred meters offshore allows for snorkeling.

activities.text: Wander the cobbled streets lined with pastel houses. Trinidad's streets haven't changed since the 19th century: no traffic lights, horse carts alongside bicycles, doors open onto interior courtyards.

Excursion by tourist train (15 km) or car through the valley, with stops at Torre Manaca Iznaga and Casa Guachinango - an 18th-century cattle-raising hacienda, built by Don Mariano Borrell, now converted into a restaurant.

Hike to the Caburní waterfall then swim in the natural pools. The trail descends through humid tropical forest - wild coffee trees, giant bamboos, 3-meter ferns. The return follows the same path (steep climb).
Beach day and snorkeling at Playa Ancón. Boat trips to the reefs are offered on site. Late afternoon, return to Trinidad for a final evening in the alleys - the Plaza Mayor bars play salsa outdoors.

map_keywords: Museo de la Lucha Contra Bandidos Trinidad

visits.text: Varadero occupies the Hicacos Peninsula, a strip of land 20 km long and 700 meters wide at its narrowest point. The white sand beach, ranked among the world's finest, runs the length of the north coast. Josone Park, a former sugar industrialist's estate from the 1940s, offers tropical gardens, a lake with rowboats and a rose garden - a peaceful haven amid the beach resort.

activities.text: Beach day at Varadero: swimming in shallow turquoise waters, snorkeling, lounging. The quietest beach stretches are toward the eastern end of the peninsula.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Cuba Circuit: Havana, Viñales, Trinidad, Santiago & Varadero

places.name: Vedado

visits.text: Return to Havana. The Museo Nacional de Bellas Artes (Cuban section), housed in a 1954 building, covers five centuries of Cuban art: colonial paintings, works by Wifredo Lam (Afro-Cuban cubism), René Portocarrero and contemporary artists. It's one of Latin America's largest art collections.

The Fortaleza de San Carlos de la Cabaña, built between 1763 and 1774 after the British capture of Havana (1762), is Latin America's largest fortress - 700 meters long. Every evening at 9 PM, soldiers in 18th-century uniform fire the traditional cannon shot (cañonazo). Che Guevara set up his office here after the 1959 Revolution.

activities.text: Late afternoon walk through the Vedado district: Art Deco and modernist architecture from the 1930s-1950s, shaded terraces along La Rampa (Calle 23). Final Havana evening facing the sea.

days_plan.itin_title: Cuba: Culture & Beach — Eco-Villages, Cenotes, Secret Cayos & Colonial Towns
Complete Cuba Circuit: Havana, Viñales, Trinidad, Santiago & Varadero

map_keywords: National Museum of Fine Arts Havana
San Carlos de la Cabaña Fortress Havana

visits.text: The Viñales Valley (UNESCO since 1999) extends over 132 square kilometers in the Sierra de los Órganos. The mogotes - limestone buttes from the Upper Jurassic (~150 million years ago) - reach 300 meters high. Their formation is unique: underground rivers carved immense caves whose vaults collapsed, leaving the walls standing. The name "Sierra de los Órganos" comes from Spanish navigators who saw in the mogotes the silhouette of organ pipes.

Visit a tobacco plantation. Pinar del Río produces ~80% of Cuban tobacco. The veguero explains each stage: sowing in September, harvesting leaf by leaf from January to March, drying 45 to 60 days in casas de tabaco - those ubiquitous wooden and palm-thatch barns. Methods remain manual: ox-drawn plows, no machinery or pesticides.

The Cueva del Indio, rediscovered in 1920, served as shelter for indigenous people before the Spanish arrival. Electrified in 1952, it's visited on foot for 300 meters (stalactites and cave paintings), then by motorboat for 400 meters along the San Vicente underground river that cuts through the mogote.

The Mural de la Prehistoria, painted on the face of Pita mogote, measures 120 m high by 180 m wide. Commissioned in 1961 by Fidel Castro, it took four years to complete by Leovigildo González Morillo, director of cartography at the Academy of Sciences. The fresco depicts the evolution of life in the Sierra: ammonites, dinosaurs, giant mammals, Guanahatabey Indians.

Climb up to the Mirador de Los Jazmines (4 km south of Viñales). The panorama embraces the entire valley: tobacco fields in the foreground, dark green mogotes with sheer flanks, Sierra de los Órganos in the background.

Cayo Jutías, an islet in the Colorados archipelago, is linked to the mainland by a pedraplén - a causeway road built over the sea, bordered by mangroves. The white sand beach stretches for 3 km facing shallow turquoise waters. Locals call it "Playa de las Estrellas" for the orange starfish visible 50 meters from shore. The island, uninhabited and hotelless, takes its name from the jutía - a large rodent endemic to Cuba living in the mangrove.

activities.text: Horseback ride among mogotes and tobacco fields. The trails cross tropical vegetation - royal palms, kapok trees, and Microcycas calocoma, a living fossil cork palm from the Cretaceous declared a national monument.

Hike through the Valle del Silencio, a rural trail among the mogotes away from tourism. The path crosses plantations of tobacco, cassava, bananas and coffee. In season (January-March), pickers work the fields.

Cuban coffee tasting and cigar-rolling demonstration at a farmer's home. The torcedor assembles the leaf by hand: wrapper, binder and filler, without glue. An experienced roller produces 100 to 150 cigars per day.

Beach day: swimming, starfish watching, snorkeling along the coral barrier reef (100 m offshore). Water sports center with kayaks and pedal boats. Bring a picnic: only one restaurant on site with variable hours.
